_metadata.description: Syllabus completeness reference — the 9 required syllabus sections (BYU-Idaho template anchor; institution-neutral except Learning Model/Vision) + a first-class generative-AI-policy requirement gate (Stoplight + AI Assessment Scale frameworks) + advisory signals. The announcement layer of the CLO → assessment → rubric chain.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~4,500 chars / 4 ≈ 1,125 tokens — within the 'embed' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Override to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~12+ knowledge files per audit task, not all. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits even for small files (matches the other knowledge files' strategy).

scope: Whether a course syllabus is COMPLETE — contains the sections students need. A completeness check, not a prose-quality judgment. Tone/clarity/'is each outcome assessed' are human judgments surfaced as advisory data, never pass/fail. Boundaries: outcome well-formedness lives in outcomes_quality_knowledge; the rubric/assessment end lives in rubrics_knowledge; the API surface lives in canvas_api_knowledge / canvas_api_lessons_learned.

facts.definition: (1) Instructor Contact Information (name, office, office hours, email, phone); (2) Overview (course description, credits, outcomes, vision — Vision is BYUI-flavored); (3) Requirements (prerequisites, materials/texts, technology, AI policy); (4) Structure (Learning Model [BYUI-specific], key assessments); (5) Expectations (feedback, workload); (6) Grading (grading scale, late-work policy); (7) Students with Disabilities (accommodation/accessibility statement); (8) University Policies (academic honesty/integrity, Title IX); (9) Disclaimers (subject-to-change / right-to-revise). Institution-neutral except Learning Model and Vision, which are BYUI-specific and flagged as such.
citations: BYU-Idaho Campus Curriculum Development syllabus template (pre_knowledge/byui_learning_teaching/byui_syllabus_guidance.md)

facts.definition: Per BYU-Idaho's AI hub, every course syllabus MUST include a statement about generative AI use. The audit treats AI-policy presence as a first-class required gate that can drive an 'incomplete' verdict on its own — distinct from the section checklist because it is an explicit institutional requirement, not a nice-to-have.
citations: BYU-Idaho AI hub, 'AI in the Syllabus' (byui.edu/ai/academics/ai-in-the-syllabus; pre_knowledge/byui_learning_teaching/byui_ai_hub.md)

facts.term: AI Policy Frameworks (Stoplight / AI Assessment Scale)
facts.definition: Two named frameworks a syllabus may use (detection of which is advisory): Stoplight Framework — RED (disallow) / YELLOW (limited use) / GREEN (active application); AI Assessment Scale — No AI → AI for Ideas → AI for Feedback → AI for Content → AI-Led. The five free-text option statements (No AI / No AI Content Creation / Cite / Open / Case-by-Case) trace to James Helfrich; the Stoplight framework is the BYU-I AI Task Force's. Evidence base: AI detectors are unreliable, so a clear operationalized policy beats policing.

facts.term: Advisory Signals (data only — never drive the verdict)
facts.definition: Syllabus bloat (pitfall #1) — very high word count is a flag, not a failure; vague expectations (pitfall #2) and dry/contractual tone (pitfall #3) — human judgments, out of deterministic scope; outcomes stated — present in syllabus text (deeper mapped+assessed check is outcomes_quality territory); Learning-Model introduced — BYUI-specific.

Section detection is keyword-heuristic on the stripped syllabus HTML. 'Not detected' means REVIEW, not proven-absent — the same evidence-based posture rubric_quality_audit.py takes on Criterion 1. The audit surfaces data for a human decision; it does not certify a syllabus as deficient.

verdict ∈ {complete, incomplete, no_syllabus} + per-section detected[] flags + ai_policy gate (present + detected framework) + advisory signals (bloat / outcomes-stated / learning-model). Emitted by syllabus_audit.py; reads syllabus_body via GET /courses/:id?include[]=syllabus_body.

facts.term: Three-Purpose Taxonomy (what a syllabus is FOR)
facts.definition: Every syllabus simultaneously serves three purposes. The 9-section checklist tells you WHAT must be in it; the three-purpose taxonomy tells you what it's FOR. (1) Inspire students — why we're in this class, how you will grow, why it matters to your future (maps to §2 Overview/Vision and FG 3.3.1 'persuasive document'). (2) Define obligations — course outcomes, course-specific policies, university-level policies (maps to §2, §3, §6, §8). (3) Outline procedures — major assignments & materials, grading scale & timelines, access to support resources (maps to §3, §4, §5, §6, §7). Use: when a syllabus passes the completeness check but feels flat, score qualitatively against the three purposes — a common failure mode is defining obligations and outlining procedures while never inspiring.
citations: BYU-I Architects of Learning Week 8 Day 1 workshop (pre_knowledge/byui_learning_teaching/byui_aol_syllabus_design_workshop_w08d1.md, slide 3)

facts.definition: Empirically-grounded dimensions surfaced as advisory only (the audit cannot reliably detect them from keyword heuristics, but each is research-cited and worth naming in operator-facing output). (1) Tone — a warm tone empowers students (Walters 2026). (2) Detail — more detailed syllabi signal that the instructor cares about the course and about students (Harrington & Gabert-Quillen 2015). (3) Framing — centering community/shared power/learning rationale, rather than rules and penalties, improves rapport (Richman et al. 2018). (4) Flexibility — flexible policies (INCLUDING penalized late work, not just no-penalty) raise student empowerment, perceived success, and belief that the instructor cares (Jones & Zhu 2022). (5) Visuals — graphics don't move perceptions of difficulty or helpfulness in isolation but they moderately correlate with a learner-centered syllabus overall (Harrington & Gabert-Quillen 2015). Posture: NOT auto-scored. Goes in advisory output as a 'things worth a human pass' reminder with citations.

Syllabus design has a ceiling effect: 'You can't paint over a bad foundation, and a fresh coat of paint won't improve a mansion' (Jones & Zhu 2022). What actually happens in the course matters far more than the syllabus ever can. Implication for the audit: treat a strong syllabus score as necessary-but-not-sufficient. A complete syllabus on a hollow course still fails students; a thin syllabus on a great course still serves them. The audit can only see the syllabus — operators should pair completeness results with course-experience signals before drawing conclusions about course quality. Adjacent caveat: self-care statements are empirically unproven but may still support student empowerment/throughput (Walters 2026) — include if voice supports it; don't require.

The BYU-I Academic Office distributes a faculty-authored Syllabus Completeness Rubric (2026) that scores syllabi against 25 specific items across 11 categories, using a 0/1/2 (missing / thin-uneven / complete) scale. More granular than the 9-section umbrella — distinguishes 'University Policies present' (umbrella) from 'Link to FERPA page present' (specific). New specific items the rubric adds: Course Information (Title/Code/Credits/Semester-year/Prerequisites = 5 items, faculty often miss Credits + specific Semester); Course Description/Outcomes 'matches catalog' alignment checks; Grading and Assessments (Weighting + Grading Scale + Exams + Projects = 4 items); Attendance policy separate from workload; AI Usage (Policy + 'right to modify' + tips for success); Additional Information umbrella; University Statements & Policies = 9 link-presence items (Personal Challenges, Disabilities, Sexual Harassment, Student Grievance, CES Honor Code, Academic Honesty, FERPA, Policy Library, Copyright disclaimer). Honest limit: keyword detection can score 0 vs ≥1 reliably; cannot reliably distinguish 1 (thin) from 2 (complete) — that's a human-judgment call.

The authoritative institutional policy on syllabus requirements. Lives on webmailbyui.sharepoint.com/sites/Policies/SitePages/3.3 Duties and Opportunities.aspx (SharePoint, auth-required). Verbatim text operator-supplied 2026-06-08. Primary purpose statement: 'The most important function of the syllabus is to help create a vision for students... a persuasive document that motivates students to learn.' Required content: course description; anticipated outcomes (may be established on department level); list of materials students need to purchase; grading and attendance policies; the final examination schedule; how/when to contact the instructor; an invitation for students with special educational needs to identify themselves; class calendar (incorporated or attached). Adds 2 items beyond the 25-item rubric: 'persuasive document / vision' framing (the audit's tone signals are out-of-scope for deterministic detection but this belongs in advisory output) and 'final examination schedule' specifically (rubric covers Exams presence but not schedule).
citations: BYU-I Faculty Guide §3.3.1 (operator-supplied verbatim text, Chaz 2026-06-08)
lib/agents/knowledge/syllabus_knowledge.md (Faculty Guide 3.3.1 verbatim block + cross-walk to 25-item rubric)

pre_run_checklist: Each entry in consumed_by[] resolves to an existing tool/agent
post_run_checklist: knowledge/syllabus_knowledge.json exists with shape=reference and facts[] present
validation.real_course_validation: DONE (2026-05-26). Exercised read-only against real non-sandbox courses: syllabus_audit run on ITM327 (402262) — correctly reported the 9-item Learning Outcomes section present; the shared outcomes parser it relies on was validated across ITM327/m119/ds250/ds460 (9/6/5/0 outcomes, no false positives). Plus the original sandbox checks (16/16 logic; live CANVAS_SANDBOX_ID read). Promoted to v1.0.

changes: Propagated v0.2 (granular 25-item rubric integration, 2026-06-08), v0.2.1 (Faculty Guide 3.3.1 verbatim block + cross-walk, 2026-06-08), and v0.3 (empirical best practices advisory layer, 2026-06-10) from the MD into the JSON facts[]. The JSON had been frozen at v0.1.1 schema-only changes while the MD evolved through v0.2/v0.2.1; this is the catch-up.

Added facts: three_purpose_taxonomy (Inspire / Define obligations / Outline procedures — source: AoL W08D1 slide 3), empirical_best_practices (5 research-backed dimensions: tone/detail/framing/flexibility/visuals — source: AoL W08D1 slide 4 + 4 cited papers), ceiling_effect_caveat (Jones & Zhu 2022 + self-care unproven caveat from Walters 2026), rubric_25_items (granular 25-item BYU-I Academic Office rubric with link-presence detection), faculty_guide_3_3_1 (canonical institutional policy + 'persuasive document / create a vision' primary purpose).

Created from the BYUI Learning & Teaching harvest (syllabus template + byui.edu/ai). Grounds syllabus_audit.py. Institution-neutral checklist with BYUI as the anchor profile; BYUI-specific items (Learning Model, Vision) flagged.

Wired into canvas_course_expert.json cross_references with a v0.x-untested caveat. Cataloged in knowledge/README.md flagged v0.1 'help us test' (deliberate exception to the v1.0-only catalog convention). Promotes to v1.0 after a real (non-sandbox) syllabus-bearing course run.

QC-audit fix: brought the JSON onto the house schema used by the other 16 knowledge files — facts converted from an object to a facts[] array (KNW-QC-003), provenance to {sources:[], last_reviewed}, _metadata gained runtime_strategy + runtime_strategy_rationale (KNW-QC-004) + template_version/generated_by/knowledge_id/owner.
